title: Adding width/height attributes to img silently disables CSS aspect-ratio
summary: Agent adds width/height attributes for CLS, which fixes both axes and makes the CSS aspect-ratio rule inert, squashing the image into a stripe.
mistake: To improve CLS, the agent adds intrinsic width/height attributes to an that is styled with width:100% and a CSS aspect-ratio (e.g. 4/5) + object-fit:cover. The height attribute now acts as the used height (both dimensions are determined), so aspect-ratio no longer applies: the image renders as a e.g. 400x1200 stripe with huge empty space. Because the attribute edit 'should not change visuals', the agent skips re-rendering the page and ships the regression; it is only caught later by an external reviewer measuring the rendered box.
instead: Whenever you add width/height attributes to styled images, also set height:auto in CSS on those images (e.g. .fig img{width:100%;height:auto}) so aspect-ratio/object-fit keep working while the attributes still reserve layout space pre-load. Treat attribute additions as visual changes: re-render and measure the element's getBoundingClientRect (or compare width/height ratio) after the edit.
